title: The Second Amendment Has a Race Problem. The Primary Source Record Documents It.
teaser: Philando Castile had a legal firearm, a valid concealed carry permit, and disclosed he was armed before he was shot seven times. The officer was acquitted. The NRA, which exists to defend licensed gun owners, issued a statement five days later. This article does not editorialize. It reads the record.
verifiedSource: Hennepin County Court Records; Minnesota Dept. of Public Safety; Urban Institute (2013); RAND Corporation Gun Policy Research; Library of Congress Black Codes Archive; NRA Public Statements Archive
body:
The Second Amendment reads: "A well regulated Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed." It does not say "the right of some people." The primary source record documents a measurable gap between the constitutional text and its applied reality, sorted by race. This article presents that record.

The Castile Case: What the Record Shows

On July 6, 2016, Philando Castile was driving in Falcon Heights, Minnesota, when officer Jeronimo Yanez pulled him over for a broken tail light. Castile had a valid Minnesota concealed carry permit. He was legally armed.

Castile informed Officer Yanez he was carrying a firearm and reached for his identification. He was shot seven times. He died at the scene. His girlfriend, Diamond Reynolds, livestreamed the immediate aftermath on Facebook. The video was viewed millions of times.

Officer Yanez was charged with second-degree manslaughter and two counts of dangerous discharge of a firearm. On June 16, 2017, he was acquitted of all charges. The City of St. Anthony subsequently reached a $3 million wrongful death settlement with Castile's family. Yanez did not return to the force.

The NRA Response Gap

The National Rifle Association positions itself as the nation's foremost defender of licensed gun owners' constitutional rights. Its standard posture when a gun owner is harmed is rapid, forceful public response.

After the Castile shooting, the NRA waited five days before issuing a statement. The statement did not call for accountability for the officer. It did not name Philando Castile as a licensed carrier whose rights were violated. It called for "all the facts" and expressed condolences. The documented contrast with the NRA's typical response posture was noted by multiple press outlets at the time.

NRA CEO Wayne LaPierre did not hold a press conference. No emergency board statement was issued. The standard machinery of second amendment defense, which activates rapidly in comparable circumstances involving non-Black gun owners, did not activate.

Stand Your Ground: What the Data Shows

Stand Your Ground laws, now in effect in over 30 states, remove the duty to retreat before using deadly force in self-defense. The laws are written without racial distinction. The outcomes are not racially neutral.

A 2013 Urban Institute analysis of FBI homicide data found that in Stand Your Ground states, white-on-Black shootings were ruled justifiable 17 percent of the time. Black-on-white shootings in the same states were ruled justifiable 1 percent of the time. Both figures come from the same law applied in the same states to the same legal standard. The 16-point gap is the documented racial outcome difference.

The RAND Corporation's Gun Policy in America research program has reviewed the body of Stand Your Ground literature and documented consistent racial outcome disparities across multiple independent studies. The finding is not one study. It is a pattern across the research record.

The Historical Record: When Gun Laws Were Designed by Race

The racial disparity in Second Amendment application did not originate in 2016. It has a documented legislative history.

Following emancipation in 1865, Southern states enacted the Black Codes, a body of state legislation that included explicit prohibitions on Black Americans owning or carrying firearms. Mississippi's 1865 statute required Black residents to obtain a license from the county police to possess a firearm. Violations meant forfeiture of the weapon and a fine. The law did not apply to white residents.

These statutes were eventually overturned by Reconstruction-era legislation. The legal architecture of race-specific firearms restrictions did not survive in statutory form. The outcome disparities documented in Stand Your Ground data and in cases like Castile's constitute the contemporary record of how that history operates without explicit statutory instruction.

Concealed Carry Permit Holders: The Documented Treatment Gap

Philando Castile was not an edge case in one specific respect: he did everything the system requires of a responsible gun owner. He obtained his permit legally. He disclosed his firearm to law enforcement. He was reaching for his identification, not his weapon. He followed protocol. The outcome was seven bullets.

The disproportionate policing of Black concealed carry permit holders is documented across multiple jurisdictions. ACLU analysis of stop-and-frisk data documents that Black and Latino residents carrying legally owned firearms were stopped, questioned, and charged at rates disproportionate to their share of the permit-holding population.

The Constitutional Question

The Second Amendment is a constitutional right. Constitutional rights do not carry asterisks. They do not vary by the race of the person asserting them. This is the foundational legal premise.

The primary source record documents a gap between that premise and the measured outcomes: in Stand Your Ground adjudications, in the institutional response to licensed Black gun owners harmed by government action, in the historical legislative record of who gun regulations were designed to target, and in the specific documented facts of July 6, 2016 in Falcon Heights, Minnesota.

Philando Castile had a legal gun. He had a valid permit. He disclosed it. He is not alive. The officer who shot him is not in prison. This is the record. No further characterization is offered here.
